sentences.base_language: Good morning! The bakery smells wonderful.
sentences.grammarNote:
**La boulangerie** = the bakery (where bread is sold); **le boulanger / la boulangère** = the baker.
**sentir bon** = to smell good — **sentir** + adverb: sentir bon (smell good), sentir mauvais (smell bad).
Note: no article before **bon** here — it functions as an adverb modifying **sentir**, not as an adjective.

sentences.base_language: I would like a baguette, please.
sentences.target_language: Je voudrais une baguette, s'il vous plaît.
sentences.grammarNote: In France, baguettes come in different styles: une baguette tradition (artisan, slightly chewy), une baguette ordinaire (standard), or une ficelle (thinner). Asking for une baguette bien cuite means well-baked and crispier.

**également** = also/as well (slightly more formal than **aussi**): et deux croissants également · **aussi** = also (informal): et deux croissants aussi.
Both are correct; **également** is often heard in shops and polite contexts.
**deux croissants** — no article needed after a number: deux croissants, trois baguettes.

sentences.base_language: Do you have pain au chocolat?
sentences.target_language: Vous avez du pain au chocolat ?
sentences.grammarNote: Du is the partitive article (de + le), used for uncountable or unspecified quantities. Here it asks 'do you have any pain au chocolat?' Compare with un pain au chocolat, which requests one specific item.

sentences.base_language: Of course! They just came out of the oven.
sentences.target_language: Bien sûr ! Ils sortent juste du four.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ils sortent juste du four** = they just came out of the oven — present tense used here to convey immediate recency ('they just came out' = 'they're coming out right now').
**du four** = from the oven — **de + le = du**.
**juste** here means 'just' (recently), not 'fair/just' as an adjective — context determines meaning.

sentences.base_language: Perfect, I'll take one pain au chocolat too.
sentences.target_language: Parfait, je prends un pain au chocolat aussi.
sentences.grammarNote:
**je prends** = I'll take — present tense used for ordering/deciding in shops. Very natural in French retail contexts.
**un pain au chocolat** = one pain au chocolat (indefinite article + specific item).
**aussi** = too/also — placed at the end of the sentence in spoken French: je prends aussi → je prends un pain au chocolat **aussi**.

sentences.base_language: Is the bread fresh this morning?
sentences.target_language: Le pain est frais ce matin ?
sentences.grammarNote:
**frais** = fresh (masculine); feminine: **fraîche** (note the circumflex and -che ending).
**Le pain est frais** — adjective after **être** agrees with the subject: le pain (masculine) → **frais**.
Question by intonation: **Le pain est frais ?** (rising tone) — the simplest question form in informal French.

sentences.base_language: Yes, we bake every morning at six.
sentences.target_language: Oui, on cuit tous les matins à six heures.
sentences.grammarNote:
**on cuit** — **cuire** (to bake/cook) is an irregular verb: je cuis, tu cuis, il/on cuit, nous cuisons.
**tous les matins** = every morning — **tous les** + masculine plural noun.
Note: the boulanger uses **on** to mean 'we' (the staff) — very natural in professional speech.

Avoir l'air + adjective means 'to look/seem': il a l'air fatigué (he looks tired), ça a l'air bon (that looks good). The adjective can agree with the subject (elle a l'air contente) or stay masculine (elle a l'air content) — both are accepted.

sentences.base_language: That comes to four euros fifty.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ça fait** = that comes to / that makes (for prices) — **faire** used for totals: ça fait dix euros, ça fait combien ?
**quatre euros cinquante** = €4.50 — in French, **cinquante** replaces '50 cents'; the word **centimes** is rarely spoken.
Common alternatives: **ça vous fait…**, **c'est…** (for prices in casual speech).

sentences.base_language: Thank you. Have a good day!
sentences.grammarNote:
**Bonne journée !** = have a good day! — used when parting, implying the day is still ahead.
Distinguish: **bonjour** (hello, greeting) vs **bonne journée** (goodbye, wishing a good day).
**bonne soirée** = have a good evening · **bonne nuit** = good night (at bedtime).
**bon week-end** = have a good weekend — **bon** is used before masculine nouns, **bonne** before feminine.
